plan.summary: Two-layer secrets protection for the tool execution path. Layer 1 (Prevention) adds sanitizedProcessEnv() to all 5 tool spawn sites, blocking credential inheritance. Layer 2 (Detection) introduces a SecretScanner that post-processes tool output using regex patterns from the existing VALUE_SECRET_PATTERNS in debug-packet.ts, emitting structured findings when secrets are detected.

in_scope: GAP-013: Other tool spawn env isolation (smart-grep, smart-git, smart-bun, project-command)
GAP-014: Bash analytics log redaction (command text)
out_of_scope: GAP-008: OPENCODE_AUTH_CONTENT bypass (workspace.ts) — requires refactoring credential passing to Effect Config
GAP-009: Provider credential injection into process.env — root cause requires SDK per-spawn env overrides
GAP-010: PTY env sanitization — separate layer
GAP-011: MCP env sanitization — separate layer
GAP-012: LSP env sanitization — separate layer
GAP-015: Enterprise storage credential loading — separate module
GAP-016: Dangerous bash pattern detection — separate hardening work
GAP-017: Bash rerouting port — separate tool work
GAP-019: OPENCODE_SERVER_PASSWORD blocklist — needs sidecar channel redesign
GAP-020: OPENCODE_STORAGE_* blocklist — needs provider module changes
shell.ts tool (shellEnv at line 419) — already constructs env via separate path

design_decisions.decision: Reuse sanitizedProcessEnv() from @opencode-ai/core/util/opencode-process rather than creating a new function
design_decisions.rationale: This function already exists and is the canonical sanitization entry point. It's used by ripgrep.ts. It delegates to sanitizeEnv() from env-blocklist.ts which applies the full ENV_BLOCKLIST + suffix patterns. Creating a parallel sanitizer would fracture the security boundary.
design_decisions.risk: depends_on_prior_fix: ENV_BLOCKLIST must contain all 31 explicit entries + 5 suffix patterns. GAP-005 (OPENCODE_AUTH_CONTENT) is already in the blocklist from RL-004.

design_decisions.decision: SecretScanner reuses VALUE_SECRET_PATTERNS regex from debug-packet.ts (line 147-155)
design_decisions.rationale: These patterns cover: bearer tokens, sk- API keys, Anthropic keys, Google API keys, AWS access keys, GitHub tokens, private key PEM blocks. They are already battle-tested in the debug export path. Copying them to a standalone module avoids coupling the tool layer to the debug module and allows independent evolution of the scanner threshold rules.
design_decisions.risk: Pattern list must stay synchronized. Mitigation: comment in both files pointing to the canonical authority.

design_decisions.decision: SecretScanner is applied at the tool output boundary — before JSON.stringify() of the output object, and before analytics log writing
design_decisions.rationale: This is the narrowest integration point: after subprocess execution, before data leaves the tool's execute() function. It catches stdout, stderr, and error messages.
design_decisions.risk: If a tool produces very large output (>5MB from smart-grep), scanning may add latency. Mitigation: scan only 10KB of stdout and 2KB of stderr by default (configurable).

design_decisions.decision: SecretScanner findings are emitted as structured metadata on the tool result, not as blocking errors
design_decisions.rationale: Blocking tool execution on secret detection could break legitimate workflows (e.g., grep finding a line containing 'sk-' in a documentation file). Instead, findings are surfaced as warnings in tool metadata so the orchestrator/agent can detect the pattern without blocking execution.
design_decisions.risk: A compromised tool chain could still exfiltrate secrets if the detection is non-blocking. Mitigation: findings are logged to a dedicated analytics stream for audit.

design_decisions.decision: Bash analytics log strips the command text, retaining only binary name and reason
design_decisions.rationale: The raw command text (line 103: cmd.slice(0, 200)) is the primary source of logged secret leakage. The binary name and agent-provided reason give sufficient signal for analytics without the command text. The command text is still preserved in the tool output returned to the agent context.
design_decisions.risk: Losing raw command from analytics reduces debugging data. Mitigation: the agent context still has the full command in metadata.command.

fixes.description: Add sanitizedProcessEnv() to smart-bash.ts spawnSync and strip command text from analytics log

changes.before:
import { Effect, Schema } from "effect"
import * as Tool from "./tool"
import { AppFileSystem } from "@opencode-ai/core/filesystem"
import { InstanceState } from "@/effect/instance-state"
import path from "path"
import { spawnSync } from "child_process"
import DESCRIPTION from "./smart-bash.txt"
changes.after:
import { Effect, Schema } from "effect"
import * as Tool from "./tool"
import { AppFileSystem } from "@opencode-ai/core/filesystem"
import { InstanceState } from "@/effect/instance-state"
import path from "path"
import { spawnSync } from "child_process"
import { sanitizedProcessEnv } from "@opencode-ai/core/util/opencode-process"
import DESCRIPTION from "./smart-bash.txt"

changes.before:
const proc = spawnSync(params.command, [], {
                  cwd,
                  encoding: "utf8" as const,
                  maxBuffer: 1024 * 1024 * 2,
                  timeout,
                  shell: true,
                  windowsHide: true,
                })
changes.after:
const proc = spawnSync(params.command, [], {
                  cwd,
                  env: sanitizedProcessEnv(),
                  encoding: "utf8" as const,
                  maxBuffer: 1024 * 1024 * 2,
                  timeout,
                  shell: true,
                  windowsHide: true,
                })

changes.before:
const logEntry = JSON.stringify({
            at: new Date().toISOString(),
            session_id: ctx.sessionID,
            agent: ctx.agent,
            binary,
            command: cmd.slice(0, 200),
            reason: params.reason,
            elapsed_ms: elapsed,
            exit_code: result.status,
          })
changes.after:
const logEntry = JSON.stringify({
            at: new Date().toISOString(),
            session_id: ctx.sessionID,
            agent: ctx.agent,
            binary,
            reason: params.reason,
            elapsed_ms: elapsed,
            exit_code: result.status,
          })

risks: Removing shell:true would break commands relying on shell expansion — but we're keeping shell:true, only adding env isolation
Some scripts may depend on env vars that are now stripped — e.g., scripts using OPENAI_API_KEY via env. These will fail gracefully with 'env var not set'. This is the intended safety behavior.
validation: bun typecheck on packages/opencode
Run smart_bash with 'echo $PATH' — should succeed
Run smart_bash with 'echo $OPENAI_API_KEY' — should output empty/nothing
Verify analytics log entry no longer has command field

risks: Git may need GIT_CONFIG or other env vars — but GIT_CONFIG is not in blocklist so it passes through. The only vars stripped are explicitly in ENV_BLOCKLIST or matching suffix patterns.
GIT_ASKPASS is in the blocklist — this is correct (it's a credential helper that shouldn't leak to subprocesses)

risks: Bun typecheck/test may need npm registry tokens (NPM_TOKEN, NPM_AUTH_TOKEN) which are explicitly blocklisted. If users have npm auth via env vars, typecheck will fail against private registries. Mitigation: document the trade-off — private registry auth should use .npmrc with token reference, not env vars.
validation: bun typecheck on packages/opencode
Run smart_bun typecheck on packages/opencode — should produce type errors normally (not auth errors)

risks: Package manager scripts (bun run, npm run) may need registry auth tokens from env vars. Same trade-off as smart-bun: private registry auth via .npmrc, not env vars.
validation: bun typecheck on packages/opencode
Run a project script via project-command — should execute normally

fixes.description: Create SecretScanner utility that post-processes tool output for secrets before returning to agent context
risks: Regex scanning large tool outputs (5MB from smart-grep) is expensive. MAX_SCAN_BYTES limits scan to first 10KB — secrets in tail sections would be missed. Acceptable trade-off: most secret exposure happens in configuration files and error messages in the head of output.
Pattern maintenance: VALUE_SECRET_PATTERNS must stay synchronized with debug-packet.ts. Add CI test or comment cross-reference.
False positives: sk- pattern matches OpenAI API keys but also legitimate documentation strings like 'sk-...' in README files. This is acceptable — the scanner redacts but doesn't block.
validation: Unit test: scanToolOutput('echo sk-ant-api03-xxx') returns finding with label 'Anthropic API key'
Unit test: scanToolOutput('echo hello world') returns no findings
Unit test: redacted output replaces sk-... with [REDACTED:sk-...]
Unit test: hasSecrets('AKIA1234567890ABCD') returns true
Unit test: hasSecrets('normal output') returns false

fixes.description: Integrate SecretScanner into smart-bash.ts tool output path

changes.before:
import { sanitizedProcessEnv } from "@opencode-ai/core/util/opencode-process"
import DESCRIPTION from "./smart-bash.txt"
changes.after:
import { sanitizedProcessEnv } from "@opencode-ai/core/util/opencode-process"
import { scanToolOutput } from "./secret-scanner"
import DESCRIPTION from "./smart-bash.txt"

changes.location: after line 143 (before return statement), insert scanner call

risks: Adding scan to every bash execution adds ~0.5ms per call on 10KB text. Negligible compared to subprocess overhead.

secret_findings field on output object may confuse downstream consumers that expect a fixed schema. Mitigation: the field is only present when secrets are found; consumers that don't recognize it will ignore it.
validation: Manual test: run smart_bash with command 'echo $OPENAI_API_KEY' (after setting to a real-looking key). Verify output has secret_findings array.
Manual test: run smart_bash with command 'echo hello'. Verify output does NOT have secret_findings.

fixes.description: Add 2 missing env vars to ENV_BLOCKLIST that slip past suffix patterns

changes.before: if (upper.endsWith('_TOKEN') || upper.endsWith('_KEY') || upper.endsWith('_SECRET') || upper.endsWith('_PASSWORD') || upper.endsWith('_CREDENTIAL')) return true
changes.after: if (upper.endsWith('_TOKEN') || upper.endsWith('_KEY') || upper.endsWith('_SECRET') || upper.endsWith('_PASSWORD') || upper.endsWith('_CREDENTIAL') || upper.endsWith('_API_KEY')) return true

notes: AWS_BEARER_TOKEN_BEDROCK: Not caught by suffix (ends with _BEDROCK, not _TOKEN/_KEY/etc.). Explicitly added.
OPENCODE_STORAGE_ACCESS_KEY_ID: Not caught by suffix (ends with _ID). Explicitly added.
AICORE_SERVICE_KEY: Already caught by _KEY suffix — no explicit addition needed.
OPENCODE_CONSOLE_TOKEN: Already caught by _TOKEN suffix — no explicit addition needed.
OPENCODE_SERVER_PASSWORD: Already caught by _PASSWORD suffix — no explicit addition needed.
OPENCODE_STORAGE_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: Already caught by _KEY suffix — no explicit addition needed.
EXA_API_KEY: Already caught by _KEY suffix — no explicit addition needed.
_API_KEY suffix added as defense-in-depth for future env vars like 'SOME_API_KEY'.
risks: Adding AWS_BEARER_TOKEN_BEDROCK to blocklist means Bedrock provider may break if it relies on process.env to find the token. The provider (provider.ts:301) writes it to process.env for SDK consumption. After this fix, the global process.env is NOT affected — only the child process env is stripped. The SDK running in-process still reads from the original process.env. This is correct behavior.

However, if the Bedrock SDK itself spawns subprocesses that inherit process.env, those subprocesses would still get the token because the SDK runs in-process. This is a deeper problem (GAP-009) that requires per-spawn env overrides, not solved by blocklist additions.
validation: Unit test: isBlocklistedEnvVar('AWS_BEARER_TOKEN_BEDROCK') returns true
Unit test: isBlocklistedEnvVar('OPENCODE_STORAGE_ACCESS_KEY_ID') returns true
Unit test: sanitizeEnv with AWS_BEARER_TOKEN_BEDROCK results in object without that key
Run smart_bash 'echo $AWS_BEARER_TOKEN_BEDROCK' — should output nothing/empty

apply_order.reason: Must be first because all subsequent sanitizedProcessEnv() calls depend on the blocklist containing the right entries. Without this, AWS_BEARER_TOKEN_BEDROCK and OPENCODE_STORAGE_ACCESS_KEY_ID would still leak to child processes.
Smart bash is the highest-risk tool (shell:true, arbitrary commands). Fix this first.
These are independent of each other — can be applied in parallel as a batch.
SecretScanner utility must exist before it can be integrated into tools.
Integrate SecretScanner into smart-bash as the detection pilot. Other tools follow in a future slice.

rollback_plan.description: Each fix is independently reversible. Revert in reverse apply order.
steps: Revert fix-G (remove secret-scanner import and scan call from smart-bash.ts)
Revert fix-E (remove env from project-command.ts Bun.spawn)
Revert fix-D (remove env from smart-bun.ts spawnSync)
Revert fix-C (remove env from smart-git.ts spawnSync)
Revert fix-B (remove env from smart-grep.ts spawnSync)
Revert fix-A (remove env from smart-bash.ts spawnSync, restore command field in analytics)
Revert fix-H (remove AWS_BEARER_TOKEN_BEDROCK and OPENCODE_STORAGE_ACCESS_KEY_ID from blocklist, remove _API_KEY suffix)

test_strategy.typecheck: bun typecheck in packages/opencode after each batch

tests: isBlocklistedEnvVar('SOME_RANDOM_API_KEY') === true (via _API_KEY suffix — wait, this is caught by _KEY suffix already. Test _API_KEY suffix specifically: 'MY_API_KEY' ends with _KEY, so already caught. Add test for 'EXA_API_KEY' which also ends with _KEY)
hasSecrets('AKIA...') returns true, hasSecrets('hello') returns false

integration_smoke: Run smart_bash 'echo hello' — succeeds, no secret_findings
Run smart_bash with inlined secret pattern 'Bearer abcdef1234567890abcdef1234567890abcdef' — succeeds, secret_findings populated
Run smart_grep on any file — succeeds
Run smart_bun typecheck on packages/opencode — succeeds (produces type errors as expected, not auth errors)
Run project-command with a project script — succeeds

consumers_of_sanitizedProcessEnv: ripgrep.ts:148 — already uses sanitizedProcessEnv(), no impact
server.ts (desktop) line 234-239 — separate createSidecarEnv, no impact
consumers_of_spawnSync_in_tools: All 4 smart_* tools and project-command.ts — all changed in this plan
affected_npm_registries: Teams using NPM_TOKEN or NPM_AUTH_TOKEN env vars for private registry auth in bun install/typecheck/test will see '401 Unauthorized' after this change. They must migrate to .npmrc with //registry.npmjs.org/:_authToken=... or use bun's native auth.
This is a known trade-off accepted for security.

future_slices.slice: SecretScanner integration into smart-grep, smart-git, smart-bun, project-command
future_slices.rationale: Pilot integration in smart-bash proves the pattern. Other tools follow the same pattern.

future_slices.slice: SecretScanner analytics — log findings to a dedicated findings stream
future_slices.rationale: Currently findings are only in tool output metadata. A dedicated analytics stream enables audit and alerting.
GAP-010 through GAP-012 cover non-tool subprocess spawns with the same env leak pattern.
Root cause: provider.ts injects credentials into global process.env. Requires per-spawn env overrides for SDKs.

code_audit_observations.observation: project-command.ts uses Bun.spawn(), not spawnSync, so Bun's env inheritance semantics apply
code_audit_observations.relevance: Bun.spawn() by default inherits full process.env when no env option is set. Adding sanitizedProcessEnv() closes this gap.

code_audit_observations.observation: shell.ts (line 419) spreads raw ...process.env into shellEnv() — separate gap that needs its own fix
code_audit_observations.relevance: Out of scope for this plan. shell.ts constructs env via a separate shellEnv() function which already has plugin-based env merging. Adding sanitization there requires understanding the plugin env contract.

code_audit_observations.observation: Existing ripgrep.ts (line 148) already uses sanitizedProcessEnv() — precedent for the pattern
code_audit_observations.relevance: Confirms the import path and function signature work correctly in the same codebase.

code_audit_observations.observation: ENV_BLOCKLIST has 31 explicit entries, covering most well-known secret env vars. The 5 suffix patterns (_TOKEN, _KEY, _SECRET, _PASSWORD, _CREDENTIAL) provide catch-all defense.
code_audit_observations.relevance: Two entries slip past suffix patterns: AWS_BEARER_TOKEN_BEDROCK (ends with _BEDROCK) and OPENCODE_STORAGE_ACCESS_KEY_ID (ends with _ID).
